quiz_questions.question_text: The 25th percentile (Q1) means that:

options.text: The value equals 25
The mean is at the 25th position
25% of data values are above this value
25% of data values fall at or below this value

quiz_questions.explanation: The kth percentile is the value below which k% of the data fall. Q1 is the 25th percentile.

quiz_questions.question_text: The interquartile range (IQR) is calculated as:

options.text: Q3 + Q1
Q2 − Q1
Maximum − Minimum
Q3 − Q1

quiz_questions.explanation: IQR = Q3 − Q1. It measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: The median is also known as:

options.text: Q1, which marks the 25th percentile of the ordered data set
Q3, which marks the 75th percentile of the ordered data set
The 25th percentile, which is the lower quartile boundary
Q2 or the 50th percentile

quiz_questions.explanation: The median splits the data in half, so it is the 50th percentile, also called Q2.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the sorted data {2, 5, 8, 11, 14, 17, 20, 23}, Q1 is:
quiz_questions.explanation: The lower half is {2, 5, 8, 11}. Q1 is the median of the lower half = (5+8)/2 = 6.5.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student scores at the 90th percentile on a standardized test. This means:

options.text: 90 students took the test
The student's score is 90
The student scored higher than 90% of test-takers
The student answered 90% of questions correctly

quiz_questions.explanation: The 90th percentile means the student's score is at or above 90% of all scores.

quiz_questions.question_text: Quartiles divide an ordered data set into:

options.text: Two equal parts
Four equal parts
One hundred equal parts
Ten equal parts

quiz_questions.explanation: Quartiles (Q1, Q2, Q3) divide ordered data into four groups of approximately equal size.

quiz_questions.question_text: If Q1 = 20 and Q3 = 45, the IQR is:
The IQR is considered a resistant measure because it:

options.text: Uses all data values in the calculation including every extreme observation
Equals the standard deviation because both measure the same type of spread
Depends only on the middle 50% of data, so outliers at the extremes don't affect it
Is always larger than the range because it covers a wider portion of data

quiz_questions.explanation: Because the IQR only involves Q1 and Q3, extreme outliers do not change its value.

quiz_questions.question_text: The five-number summary consists of:

options.text: Mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation
Minimum, Q1, Median, Q3, Maximum
First five data values

quiz_questions.explanation: The five-number summary is: Min, Q1, Median (Q2), Q3, Max. It forms the basis of a boxplot.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deciles divide data into how many equal parts?
quiz_questions.explanation: Deciles divide data into 10 equal parts. The 1st decile is the 10th percentile, the 2nd decile is the 20th percentile, and so on.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a percentile?

options.text: A percentage of correct answers on a test
A type of graph
The same as the mean
A value below which a given percentage of data falls

quiz_questions.explanation: The kth percentile is the value at or below which k% of the observations in a data set fall.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a quartile?

options.text: One of three values (Q1, Q2, Q3) that divide an ordered data set into four equal parts
A quarter of the data set, meaning exactly 25% of the total observations
The fourth value in the data set when arranged in ascending numerical order
A measure of central tendency that describes the typical value in a data set

quiz_questions.explanation: The three quartiles Q1, Q2, and Q3 split an ordered data set into four groups, each containing about 25% of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the interquartile range (IQR)?

options.text: The standard deviation of the quartiles, calculated from Q1, Q2, and Q3 values
The range of the entire data set, computed as the maximum minus the minimum value
The difference between the third quartile and the first quartile (Q3 − Q1), representing the spread of the middle 50% of data
The average of Q1 and Q3, which gives the midpoint of the middle fifty percent

quiz_questions.explanation: IQR = Q3 − Q1. It captures the spread of the central 50% of the data and is resistant to outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the five-number summary?

options.text: The first five values in a data set
Mean, median, mode, range, and standard deviation
Five randomly selected data points
A set of five statistics: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum that summarize a data set's distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: The five-number summary (Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max) provides a compact description of the distribution and is used to construct boxplots.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'percentile rank' mean?

options.text: The rank of the highest value in the data set
The number of data points in the data set
The percentage of data values that are less than or equal to a given value
The percentage of values that equal the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: The percentile rank of a value tells you what percentage of the data set falls at or below that value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a decile?

options.text: The 10th value in a data set
A value that divides a data set into 4 equal parts
A type of decimal number
A value that divides a data set into 10 equal parts

quiz_questions.explanation: Deciles divide data into 10 equal groups. The first decile equals the 10th percentile, the fifth decile equals the median, etc.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 1.5 × IQR rule identify?

options.text: The mean of the data set
The number of quartiles
Potential outliers: values below Q1 − 1.5×IQR or above Q3 + 1.5×IQR
The standard deviation

quiz_questions.explanation: The 1.5×IQR rule sets fences at Q1 − 1.5×IQR and Q3 + 1.5×IQR. Any data point outside these fences is considered a potential outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cumulative percentage' mean in a percentile context?

options.text: The total of all data values
The running total of percentages up to and including a given class or value
The percentage of outliers
The percentage of the highest class only

quiz_questions.explanation: Cumulative percentage is the running total of relative frequencies, showing what fraction of data falls at or below each value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a percentile and a percentage?

options.text: They are the same thing
A percentage is always larger than a percentile
A percentile is a data value marking a position in the distribution; a percentage is a proportion out of 100
A percentile is a type of graph; a percentage is a number

quiz_questions.explanation: A percentile indicates relative standing (e.g., 'this score is at the 75th percentile'), while a percentage simply represents a fraction of 100 (e.g., '75% correct').

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'outlier fence' in the context of quartiles?

options.text: The minimum and maximum of the data, representing the most extreme observed values
The standard deviation limits, computed by adding and subtracting from the mean value
The range of the data set, calculated as the maximum value minus the minimum value
The boundary values calculated using Q1 − 1.5×IQR (lower fence) and Q3 + 1.5×IQR (upper fence) beyond which data points are considered outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: Outlier fences are computed from the quartiles and IQR. Points beyond these fences are flagged as potential outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pediatrician measures a baby's weight at 8.2 kg and reports it is at the 60th percentile for babies of that age. What does this mean?

options.text: 60% of babies the same age weigh 8.2 kg or less
60 babies were measured
The baby is overweight
The baby weighs 60% of the ideal weight

quiz_questions.explanation: Being at the 60th percentile means the baby's weight exceeds or equals that of 60% of same-age babies.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data set of 12 home prices (in thousands) sorted in order is: 150, 175, 190, 210, 225, 240, 255, 270, 290, 310, 350, 400. Find Q1, Q3, and the IQR.
Using the home price data from the previous question (Q1=200, Q3=300, IQR=100), determine the outlier fences. Is the $400k home an outlier?

options.text: Lower fence = 200, Upper fence = 300; $400k IS an outlier
There are no outlier fences for this data
Lower fence = 50, Upper fence = 450; $400k is NOT an outlier
Lower fence = 100, Upper fence = 400; $400k IS an outlier

quiz_questions.explanation: Lower fence = Q1 − 1.5×IQR = 200 − 150 = 50. Upper fence = Q3 + 1.5×IQR = 300 + 150 = 450. Since 400 < 450, the $400k home is not an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a class of 200 students, a student scored 156th when ranked from lowest to highest. What is the student's approximate percentile rank?

options.text: 96th percentile
56th percentile
22nd percentile
78th percentile

quiz_questions.explanation: Percentile rank ≈ (156/200) × 100 = 78th percentile. The student scored higher than about 78% of the class.

quiz_questions.question_text: A company's employee salaries have Q1 = $45,000, Q2 = $58,000, Q3 = $72,000. An executive earns $150,000. Is this salary an outlier by the 1.5×IQR rule?

options.text: No, because $150,000 is below the upper fence of $112,500
Yes, because $150,000 is more than double the median
No, because executives are always excluded from outlier analysis
Yes, because $150,000 exceeds the upper fence of $112,500

quiz_questions.question_text: A fitness test records push-up counts for 20 recruits. The sorted data yields Q1 = 22, Median = 30, Q3 = 38. A recruit does 55 push-ups. The drill sergeant says this is an 'extreme outlier.' Using the 3×IQR rule for extreme outliers, is the sergeant correct?

options.text: No, because there are no outliers in fitness data
Yes, because any value above Q3 is extreme
Yes, because 55 > Q3 + 3×IQR = 86
No, because the extreme outlier fence is Q3 + 3×IQR = 38 + 48 = 86, and 55 < 86

quiz_questions.explanation: IQR = 38−22 = 16. Extreme outlier fence = Q3 + 3×IQR = 38 + 48 = 86. Since 55 < 86, the value is not an extreme outlier (though it may be a mild outlier by the 1.5×IQR rule since 38+24=62 > 55... actually 55 < 62, so it's not even a mild outlier).

quiz_questions.question_text: Two hospitals compare patient wait times. Hospital A: Q1=10 min, Q3=30 min. Hospital B: Q1=18 min, Q3=22 min. Which hospital has more consistent wait times in the middle 50%?

options.text: Hospital B, because its IQR of 4 minutes is much smaller than Hospital A's IQR of 20 minutes
They are equally consistent
Hospital A, because it starts lower
Hospital A, because its Q3 is higher

quiz_questions.explanation: Hospital A IQR = 30−10 = 20 min. Hospital B IQR = 22−18 = 4 min. Hospital B's middle 50% of patients experience much more consistent wait times.

quiz_questions.question_text: A standardized test reports scores in percentiles. Student A is at the 85th percentile and Student B is at the 90th percentile. A parent concludes that Student B scored 5 percentage points higher. Is this conclusion correct?

options.text: Yes, because percentiles are linear scales
No, because the 90th percentile is always exactly twice the 45th
No, percentiles indicate relative standing, not raw score differences; the actual score gap could be large or small
Yes, percentiles directly correspond to score differences

quiz_questions.explanation: Percentiles reflect rank position, not score magnitude. Near the extremes, a small percentile difference may correspond to a large or small score gap depending on the distribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data analyst reports that a city's household income has Q1=$35k, Q2=$50k, Q3=$75k. A journalist writes: 'Half of all households earn between $35k and $75k.' Is this accurate?

options.text: No, it only covers 25% of households
Yes, that is exactly what Q1 and Q3 tell us
No, quartiles only apply to test scores
No, Q1 to Q3 covers 75% of households

quiz_questions.explanation: Q1 marks the 25th percentile and Q3 marks the 75th percentile, so the interval from Q1 to Q3 contains the middle 50% (half) of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marathon records finishing times (minutes) for 1,000 runners. The five-number summary is: Min=145, Q1=210, Median=240, Q3=275, Max=420. The race director wants to award medals to runners finishing in the top 25%. What is the approximate cutoff time?

options.text: 210 minutes (Q1), since the fastest 25% finish at or below Q1
240 minutes (the median)
275 minutes (Q3)
145 minutes (the minimum)

quiz_questions.explanation: The fastest 25% are the runners with the lowest times, i.e., below Q1 = 210 minutes. Q1 marks the boundary below which 25% of runners fall.
